audit_status.source_base: Greek Gospel text tradition, draft aligned to public-domain comparison layer later
audit_status.review_note: Compact complete draft reviewed for Pilate/Herod trials, Barabbas, daughters of Jerusalem, crucifixion, forgiveness textual variant, criminals, death, centurion, women witnesses, Joseph burial.

verses.restored: The whole multitude of them rose and led him to Pilate.
They began to accuse him, saying, We found this one misleading our nation, forbidding tribute to Caesar, and saying he himself is Messiah, a king.
Pilate questioned him, saying, Are you the king of the Judeans? Answering him, he said, You say it.
Pilate said to the chief priests and crowds, I find no cause in this human.
But they were insisting, saying, He stirs up the people, teaching through all Judea, beginning from Galilee even to here.
Pilate, hearing this, asked whether the person was Galilean.
Learning that he was from Herod's authority, he sent him up to Herod, who was also in Jerusalem in those days.
Herod, seeing Jesus, rejoiced greatly, for he had long wanted to see him because of hearing about him, and he hoped to see some sign happen through him.
He questioned him with many words, but Jesus answered him nothing.
The chief priests and scribes stood there, vehemently accusing him.
Herod with his soldiers treated him as nothing, mocked him, clothed him in bright clothing, and sent him back to Pilate.
Herod and Pilate became friends with one another that day; before this they had been in hostility with each other.
Pilate called together the chief priests, rulers, and people,
and said to them, You brought me this person as one turning the people away. Look, having examined him before you, I found in this person no cause for the charges you bring against him.
Neither did Herod, for he sent him back to us. Look, nothing worthy of death has been done by him.
Therefore, after disciplining him, I will release him.
[Some later manuscripts add that he had obligation to release one to them at the feast.]
But they cried out all together, saying, Take this one away, and release Barabbas to us.
He had been thrown into prison because of an uprising in the city and murder.
Pilate addressed them again, wanting to release Jesus.
But they kept shouting, Crucify, crucify him.
A third time he said to them, Why, what harm did this one do? I found no cause of death in him. Therefore, after disciplining him, I will release him.
But they pressed with loud voices, asking that he be crucified, and their voices prevailed.
Pilate decided that their demand should happen.
He released the one thrown into prison for uprising and murder, whom they asked for, but Jesus he handed over to their will.
As they led him away, taking hold of Simon of Cyrene coming from the country, they laid the cross on him to carry behind Jesus.
A great multitude of the people and women followed him, mourning and lamenting for him.
Turning to them, Jesus said, Daughters of Jerusalem, do not weep for me; rather weep for yourselves and for your children.
For look, days are coming in which they will say, Blessed are the barren, the wombs that did not bear, and breasts that did not nurse.
Then they will begin to say to the mountains, Fall on us, and to the hills, Cover us.
For if they do these things when the wood is green, what will happen when it is dry?
Two others also, criminals, were led with him to be executed.
When they came to the place called Skull, there they crucified him and the criminals, one at his right and one at his left.
[Jesus said, Father, release them, for they do not know what they are doing.] They divided his garments, casting lots.
The people stood watching. The rulers were sneering, saying, He saved others; let him save himself, if this is the Messiah of the Divine, the Chosen One.
The soldiers also mocked him, coming near and offering sour wine,
and saying, If you are the king of the Judeans, save yourself.
There was also an inscription over him: This is the king of the Judeans.
One of the criminals hanging there blasphemed him, saying, Are you not the Messiah? Save yourself and us.
But the other answered, rebuking him, saying, Do you not even fear the Divine, since you are under the same judgment?
We indeed justly, for we receive what is worthy of what we did; but this one did nothing out of place.
He said, Jesus, remember me when you come into your reign.
He said to him, Amen, I say to you: today you will be with me in Paradise.
It was already about the sixth hour, and darkness came over the whole land until the ninth hour,
the sun failing; and the curtain of the temple was torn in the middle.
Calling out with a great voice, Jesus said, Father, into your hands I entrust my spirit. Saying this, he breathed out.
When the centurion saw what happened, he glorified the Divine, saying, Truly this human was righteous.
All the crowds that had gathered for this spectacle, seeing the things that happened, returned beating their chests.
All those who knew him stood far away, and the women who followed him from Galilee, seeing these things.
Look, a man named Joseph, a council member, a good and righteous man,
who had not consented to their counsel and action, from Arimathea, a city of the Judeans, who was waiting for the reign of the Divine;
this one came to Pilate and asked for the body of Jesus.
Taking it down, he wrapped it in linen and placed him in a tomb cut in stone, where no one had yet been laid.
It was Preparation day, and Sabbath was dawning.
The women who had come with him from Galilee followed and saw the tomb and how his body was placed.
Returning, they prepared spices and ointments. On the Sabbath they rested according to the command.
